proposal_text:
Proposal number: 1333885

Proposal: LET'S WALK! - climate change awareness day event

Summary


Purpose: helping to rise the climate change awareness our world so urgently needs. 

Suggested actions: During that day we encourage people to walk and to:

leave their cars at home,
skip the bus,
avoid the subway and
turn to their own bodies in order to move around the city, by walking, by using their bicycles, their rollers, their skates.
If you are not lucky enough to be living near your work space you can still collaborate by doing home office and other activities such as walking to the grocery or the club.
Using a smartphone app we intend to track how much people walk during the day of the event. This information will then be published by social media and traditional media. The data provided by the app will also be translate in the reductions already mentioned, calculating savings in relationship  with the kilometrers walked and avoiding using transportation during that day.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page [14].
Because of the altruistic spirit of the cause, we expect to have support from the local ONG´s, health organizations and the government.     
Main value generation loops v1.0.png


Short term Impacts: we expect to save energy and money, GHG emissions, and we will contribute to build sustainable habits, for a healthy environment in the war against the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle.  

Long term impacts:

Since the proposal is to make people aware of how important it is to care about the planet. More climate change aware people would naturally support the following initiatives:

Recycling: We forecast that it could increase by 10 %,
 
Energy savings: recommendations use increment by 10 %
 
Distributed solar photovoltaic electricity law: is approved and covers 5 % of city demand.
 
Electric buses: their use is implemented in 2 % of city bus lines.
Is this proposal for a practice or a project?
Practice

What actions do you propose?
a) The main thrust of “Let’s Walk” is walking, because Buenos Aires has a high population density, and distances might be shorter than in other cities, so walking to work is a real possibility for some people. People can

Walk 10 - 15 blocks to their offices or back home.

Use public transportation, including buses, underground / subways and then walk the rest of the way to their offices or homes.

 

b) Cycling also adds value to the “Let’s walk” proposal for the population 10 to 30 blocks from their office. The Buenos Aires City already has implemented an “ecobici” (eco bikes) program [15]. The ecobici system has bikes parked in several distributed city points, and anyone can use one of these bikes for free using his / her document. You just return the bike in another bike parking place near your destination.

 

REVIEW OF SOME “BIKE TO WORK” PROGRAMS IN CANADA AND USA:

USA Today informs that Bike to Work programs might be rising in US [21].

Through a google search we found the following relevant links:

table-bike-to-work-projects.png


ANSWERS TO SOME OBJECTIONS TO “BIKE TO WORK”

The Huffpost article [16] mentions 9 objections to “Bike to work”, here are our thoughts about them!

main-objections.png

“LET’S WALK BUSINESS MODEL CANVAS”

LET`S WALK PROJECT BUSINESS CANVAS v2.0.png

As we know we have different partners in this event, which play a fundamental role.

On one side, the people enrolled in the event, that will participate registering using a mobile app. They will either walk to their works or commute by bike. In order to be eligible to receive a prize, each participant will allow the app to register information about distances walked / biked, which transportation they usually use to commute in order to calculate GHG emission savings due to the event. Those people that contribute with the biggest GHG emission savings will win prizes as well as  benefits from companies that sponsor the event.

It´s necessary to distinguish, that by incentivizing the movement we are preventing obesity, decrease bad cholesterol and increase good cholesterol, improving the state of mind and incorporating healthy habits, so typically HMOs and the city government will be very much interested, as in Argentina poor people use public hospitals maintained by city taxes.

On the other side, we need to be able to organize this event with which we need the Government's support to convey the event to the society. It will be benefited by an increased use of the public transport by the population, better flow of the traffic (less cars in the streets)  and at the same time it will reaffirm it´s commitment with the environment.

In addition to this, it will be a great opportunity to advertise the use of the eco-bikes(“ecobicis”) [15] and, as a result, the bike lanes.

On the other hand, the information generated by the app (big data)  will be of great utility since it will show which are the population's needs (for example: the best locations for entrances of subways , routes of buses or even which are the areas that are disconnected and need public transport).

It is also important to have the companies that will join on this great event allowing the use of “home office”. In addition, they will collaborate as sponsors and obtain in exchange Big Data to improve its business and find niche.

Sponsorships are one of the best ways of promoting a business and winning popularity between the target and generate good marketing for a brand. It can be specially used to increase credibility, image and prestige of the brand.

All participation is voluntary, and public transportation will still be available to those who want to use it.

LGC Team will ask for suggestions and advice from NGOs(such as WWF, who implemented "The earth hour"). In order to have more tools to guide the team on how to develop the project. 
 
Once LCG Team recieves feedback from NGOs.They will present the formal proposal to the Institute of Technology of Buenos Aires (ITBA) and to the MIT Colab
 
The team is looking forward to receive feedback from CLIMATE COLAB in order to improve the project. ITBA will give their consent and support to it.
 
We received the recognition and prize from MIT Colab thanks to the backing through votes from ITBA.
 
LGC Team will contact several organizations to help with fundraising and to add value to the final event (GOOGLE, MASS MEDIA, SPONSORS, etc). Nevertheless to make it happen we need firstly the GOVERNMENT authorization.
 
The government will notificate the companies of the proposal and ask for their cooperation by allowing the employees to work from home. They will also have to arrange with the transportation about the modification of their routes.
 
Distance walked and GHG savings: To have an estimation of the GHGs saved is necessary an app (develop by GOOGLE if possible) that will measure them.The application may use a similar alogorithm that "calculate your ecological footprint" uses, that is available at the Government web page.But it is important to keep record of the route like Google Maps does. 
 
Once the proposal is accepted the next step is to settle a date and communicate it to the society, a key actor in this process is MASS MEDIA. 
 
The day of the event SPONSORS will play an important role by motivating the population to walk more. This could be achived by offering refreshments and prizes. 
 
Finally,all the information is collected by GOOGLE. Including steps per person and the equivalence with the reduction of GHGs obteined.
 
The LGC Team will analyze the results and make papers to be published and to communicate it to the community, the government and the MIT. 
 
As the results will be positive this idea can be implemented in other countries, all over the world. By this the proposal will reach a greater impact and a change in people's habits worldwide.


Who will take these actions?
LGC Team at ITBA: designs proposal and presents it to Climate Colab.
 
Argentine Government sponsors "LET'S WALK day"
 
Media covers the events publishing metrics on energy saved, emissions saved and money saved.
 
5 % local city population participates in the "LET'S WALK day"
 
NGOs sponsor event.
 
Transportation works as usual.

Other countries can implement the proposal to have a higher impact
Where will these actions be taken?
City level: The first objective is to do it in Buenos Aires City as is the most populated region, the most contaminating one and the principal concentration of companies.

Country level: Later by the positive results our goal is to carry out the experience at country level, in cities like Cordoba, Rosario, Mendoza and San Luis.

Worldwide: Finally, other countries can use the proposal to make their own impacts since everything counts.

 

In addition, specify the country or countries where these actions will be taken.
Argentina

Country 2
Brazil

Country 3
Chile

Country 4
United States

Country 5
No country selected

Impact/Benefits
What impact will these actions have on greenhouse gas emissions and/or adapting to climate change?
1) City of Buenos Aires

a) Short term CO2e emissions saved in Buenos Aires city:

We postulate that 5 % of the population will participate in the event.
Public transportation use: 80% of population [1]

 
Emissions saved by cars commuting from Buenos Aires Province:

10410352 cars/month [2] /30 days/month x 15 km/trip  x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] x 0.05 ( 5 % population participates  in event) = 26 tCO2e (metric tons) / day.
 

Emission saved by cars inside the city:

2.89 million people x 5% people participation x 20 % people use cars x 7 km average trip to work x 2 trips /day x 0.1 kg CO2eq / km [3] = 40.46 tCO2e / day
 

Total short term CO2e savings = (26 + 40.46) = 66.46 tCO2e /day

b) Long term CHG savings (5 years) in Buenos Aires city:

We forecast that recycling could increase by 10 %. Today only 23% of Buenos Aires city recycle [4]. And recycling saves from 0.34 to 10 Tonnes of CO2e from each ton of recycled material [5]. Considering an average 1 Tonne of CO2e saved per Tonne of recycled material, current Buenos Aires savings from recycling would be 976 Tonnes of domiciliary residues [6] x 23% people recycle x 0.1 (10 % recycling increase ) x 1 tCO2e/1 t recycled material = 22 Tonnes of CO2e / year saved.
 
Energy savings recommendations [7] use increment by 10 %. The average electricity consumption per electrified household in Argentina is 3600 kWh [11], so we estimate that the savings will be of 360 kWh/home per year * 963 thousand homes * 0.05 (5 % of participation) = 17334 kWh per year. This could save up to 17334 kWh x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 10.2 tCO2e/year
Distributed solar photo voltaic electricity law is approved and covers 1 % of city demand.
2.89 million people x 4100 kWh/person [10] x 0.01 (1% of demand) x 0.0005925 tCO2e/kWh = 70205 tCO2e/year
 
It incentivates the use of public transport in this way, so the Government must make it more efficient.For example, the use of electric buses is implemented in 2 % of city.[8] [9] Each bus will save 175 metrics tones of pollutant emissions and 22.700 liters of fuel per unit and year. We estimate that there are 10200 buses in Buenos Aires,so 2% is 204, the total reduction will be 204*175 mtones= 35700 metric tones of pollutant / year saved.[10]
 
Total emission savings = (22 + 10.2 + 70205 + 35700) = 105937 tCO2e/year

2) Extrapolation to world:

a) Short term:

66.46 tCO2e/(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 24997 tCO2e

b) Long term:

105.937 tCO2e/year /(2.89million people) x (1087 million people) = 39 million tCO2e / year.

The transportation sector accounts from 19 % to 25 % of total GHGs emissions, according to [12] and [13]

So the total GHG due to the transportation sector may be calculated as

46.000 million tCO2e x 22% = 10.120 million tCO2e

So the proposal savings would be 39 / 10120 = 0.38 % of the transportation sector emissions.

What are other key benefits?
Generate awareness on using more public transport
Government will have to improve the efficiency of public transport
Change the habits of the society towards a sustainable and ecological life
Smog and sound pollution will be reduced
People´s stress levels will be reduced
Increase the use of bicycles
Change habits and weaken sedentary lifestyle
Cut costs of transport and so combustibles
By implemeting home-office besides helping this cause, the employeers will have a cut down of their costs
Decrease GHGs emissions by reducing the use of cars and public transport
Costs/Challenges
What are the proposal’s projected costs?
1) Cost of security forces involved in organizing the event.

2) Cost of health support personnel, ambulances, paramedics,

3) Cost of traditional media ads

4) Cost of blog, SN ads and  social media managers / influencers

5) Cost of winner prizes

6) Cost of collaboration agreement with smartphone apps that calculate distances walked by people participating in the event

7) Cost of webpage that summarizes energy savings, money saved, GHGs emission saved.

Timeline
The day of the event, there will be an immediate impact by the reduction of the GHGs emissions.
In the short-term we are looking forward to make people realize that their can make a huge change on a daily basis.For example, they can avoid the use of cars in short distances.
Over more, we can affirm that one of important change will be the use bikes. Nowadays, Buenos Aires city provides free bikes and bicicles in the downtown. Our idea is to massify these kind of habits and became a green country free of sedentarism.
What's more, we want to Incentive companies to stablish a home-office day. This will allow people who live far from their jobs to colaborate to this cause.
Medium-term: These "Homeoffice days" could at first be once a year, but once people catch the habit, it could be done monthly.
Long-term: Eventually people could do these "Homeoffice days" weekly, so that people enjoy life a little more and savings of energy, money and GHGs emissions are multiplied.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event while simultaneously abandoning public transit (bus/subway) for walking or cycling. The causal mechanism for long-term impact (e.g., a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws) is tenuous; there is no empirical evidence provided that a one-day awareness event can drive complex legislative or behavioral shifts of this magnitude. Furthermore, the technical feasibility of the proposed app is low, as it requires integration with Google Maps and a custom GHG algorithm without a defined development roadmap or data privacy strategy.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally, with numerous existing programs in the US, Canada, and Europe cited in the text itself. The proposal adds a layer of 'Big Data' tracking via a smartphone app, but the technical approach (using existing footprint algorithms) is not novel. The suggestion to skip public transit entirely in favor of walking for short distances is a standard behavioral nudge, not a systemic innovation.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically consistent with the stated assumptions but likely overestimates actual emission reductions due to the 'rebound effect' and the unlikelihood of mass transit abandonment. The long-term impact projections are scientifically implausible; attributing a 10% increase in recycling, the passage of specific solar legislation, and the deployment of electric buses to a single awareness event represents a severe overestimation of causal efficacy. The extrapolation to a global scale (39 million tCO2e/year) is based on a linear scaling of a one-day event, which ignores systemic constraints and diminishing returns.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing for its claims. However, the presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and calculation errors (e.g., the long-term impact section conflates the impact of the event with the impact of unrelated policy changes like the solar law). The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without textual explanation reduces the clarity of the argument. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous hedging required for scientific plausibility.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal presents a classic case of 'awareness fallacy,' where a single event is expected to trigger complex, long-term systemic changes (legislation, infrastructure shifts) without a defined causal mechanism. While the short-term behavioral nudge is plausible, the quantitative impact projections are scientifically unsound due to unrealistic participation rates and the attribution of macro-policy outcomes to a micro-event. The technical implementation of the data tracking app is also underspecified.
key_concerns: The assumption that 5% of the population will abandon public transit for walking/cycling on a single day lacks empirical support and ignores urban mobility constraints.

The causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, 10% recycling increase) is not scientifically established and likely represents a conflation of correlation and causation.
The 'Big Data' value proposition is vague; the proposal does not address data privacy, API costs, or the technical feasibility of real-time GHG calculation at scale.
The long-term impact calculations appear to attribute the success of broader city-wide initiatives (e.g., electric bus rollout) to this specific event, inflating the perceived impact.

The proposal suggests skipping public transit, which may inadvertently increase congestion if participants drive to the city center to walk, or simply fail to materialize due to weather, distance, or safety concerns.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers. It assumes the government can mandate or effectively coordinate 'home office' for private companies and modify public transport routes for a single day without a formal legislative or regulatory framework. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom app and manage all data collection is operationally speculative; large tech firms rarely commit resources to ad-hoc, one-off civic events without a commercial or strategic partnership. The governance capacity required to synchronize NGOs, media, transport authorities, and private employers for a single-day event is not demonstrated.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific application of a smartphone app for real-time GHG tracking and the attempt to link this data to long-term policy outcomes (like solar law adoption). However, the core mechanism is not innovative, and the governance model proposed (voluntary participation + government endorsement) is standard for such awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of a distributed solar law as direct results of a single walking event. From a policy perspective, there is no mechanism described that translates 'awareness' into legislative change or systemic infrastructure shifts (e.g., electric bus implementation). The short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are negligible in the context of city-wide emissions, and the extrapolation to global impacts (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically flawed as it assumes a single-day behavioral change can be sustained and scaled globally without addressing structural barriers.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts, Costs), but the presentation of the governance strategy is weak. It lists stakeholders (Government, NGOs, Google, Media) but fails to define their specific roles, responsibilities, or the legal/contractual frameworks required to bind them. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') and the lack of a detailed implementation roadmap for the 'government authorization' phase reduce the professional quality of the submission.

A2.perspective_summary: This proposal presents a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates the power of a single event to drive systemic policy change. While the intent to promote active transport is sound, the governance model is fragile, relying on voluntary cooperation from powerful entities (Google, private employers) and assuming a direct causal link between a one-day event and complex legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification). Without a robust institutional framework to enforce or incentivize participation and a realistic data strategy, the proposal remains a theoretical exercise rather than an actionable policy intervention.
key_concerns: Lack of a clear regulatory or contractual mechanism to secure 'home office' participation from private companies.
Unrealistic assumption that a single event can trigger long-term legislative changes (e.g., solar law approval) without a dedicated advocacy or lobbying strategy.
Dependency on Google for app development and data management without a confirmed partnership or funding model.
Absence of a risk management plan for public safety and traffic disruption during the event.
The 'Big Data' value proposition is vague; it is unclear how the government would legally access or utilize this data for urban planning without privacy regulations.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with unrealistic dependencies: government authorization to modify bus routes, Google developing a custom tracking app, and mass corporate adoption of home office for a one-off day. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and media for a city-wide walk without a clear pilot phase or budget breakdown makes immediate execution highly risky.

novelty.rationale: The core concept (walking/biking to work days) is a well-established global practice (e.g., 'Bike to Work Day'). The proposal adds little novelty beyond aggregating existing ideas (Ecobici, home office) and proposing a generic tracking app. The 'business model' is essentially a sponsorship drive, which is standard for such events.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e/year) are mathematically flawed due to unrealistic extrapolation assumptions (e.g., assuming a one-day event triggers permanent 10% recycling increases and 2% electric bus fleet conversion). The proposal conflates a single event's awareness with systemic infrastructure changes that require years of policy work, not a single day of walking.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the data analysis is superficial and the logic connecting the event to long-term policy outcomes is weak. The inclusion of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Review of Objections' shows effort, but the reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and unverified assumptions undermines the professional presentation.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that overestimates its ability to drive systemic change. While the intent is positive, the operational plan is fragile, relying on high-level government and corporate cooperation that is unlikely to materialize for a single day without a proven track record. The financial and logistical burden of security, health support, and app development for a one-off event is high relative to the minimal, short-lived emissions reduction.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes government will modify public transport routes and companies will mandate home office for a single day without a clear incentive structure or legal framework.
The long-term impact calculations are mathematically unsound, projecting permanent policy changes (e.g., electric bus adoption) as a direct result of a one-day awareness event.
There is no clear budget or funding strategy beyond vague 'sponsorships' and 'government support,' making the cost of security, health personnel, and app development a major risk.
The reliance on a custom app developed by Google (or similar) for tracking is a significant technical and partnership barrier that is not addressed with a realistic timeline or cost estimate.
The proposal lacks a pilot phase; scaling from a single day to a global movement without testing the operational model in a smaller context is a high-risk strategy.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'government authorization' to modify bus routes and mandate home office days, which is a massive bureaucratic hurdle for a single-day event. Furthermore, the requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific routes and calculate GHG savings creates a high technical barrier that ignores existing, accessible tools (like Strava or Google Fit) that already serve this purpose, likely excluding those without smartphones or data plans.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice. While the proposal attempts to gamify it with prizes and data tracking, the core mechanism is not novel. The specific claim of using 'big data' to redesign subway entrances is a standard urban planning outcome of existing mobility studies, not a unique innovation of this event.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact of a single day of walking is negligible (66 tCO2e) and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e) rely on highly optimistic, unproven assumptions about habit formation (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar adoption) triggered by one event. However, the potential for immediate health benefits and localized awareness in Buenos Aires is real, even if the global scaling claims are inflated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is fragmented and contains significant logical gaps. It explicitly asks people to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' to walk, which contradicts the goal of sustainable transport for those living far away. The text is riddled with typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm'), inconsistent formatting, and a confusing flow between the 'event' and 'long-term policy changes' that are not directly caused by the event itself.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down campaign that misunderstands the daily realities of Buenos Aires residents. While the intent to promote walking is positive, the instruction to 'skip the bus' is dangerous and impractical for anyone with a commute longer than a few blocks. The heavy reliance on a custom app and government mandates ignores the accessibility needs of the elderly, disabled, and low-income populations who cannot easily walk long distances or afford smartphones. The proposal risks alienating the very people it aims to help by setting unrealistic expectations for a one-day event to solve systemic transport and climate issues.
key_concerns: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is exclusionary and potentially unsafe for residents with long commutes, disabilities, or mobility issues.
The proposal assumes universal smartphone ownership and data access for the tracking app, creating a digital divide that excludes vulnerable populations.
The reliance on government mandates for home office and route changes is a major feasibility risk that is not addressed with a contingency plan.
The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are causally disconnected from a single-day walking event and lack a realistic behavioral change mechanism.
The proposal does not account for safety concerns (traffic, crime) for pedestrians in specific neighborhoods of Buenos Aires, which could deter participation.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly enforce 'home office' and route modifications for public transport, which contradicts the voluntary nature of the event. Furthermore, the logistical burden of providing security and health support for a city-wide walking event without a clear operational plan for crowd control or emergency response in a dense urban environment like Buenos Aires presents significant execution risks.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the integration of a specific app for tracking and the 'Big Data' angle for urban planning adds a layer of modernity, the fundamental behavioral intervention and the value generation loops are derivative of existing campaigns (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work programs cited in the text).

impact.rationale: The proposal identifies clear potential benefits (GHG reduction, health improvements, traffic flow), but the projected long-term impacts (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are based on a linear extrapolation of a single-day event that ignores behavioral decay and systemic barriers. The immediate impact is real but likely limited to the 5% participation rate, and the proposal fails to address the 'rebound effect' or the exclusion of those who physically cannot walk or bike, limiting the net positive impact on equity.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant inconsistencies in its calculations (e.g., the jump from 1% to 5% solar coverage in the long-term section without explanation) and a lack of critical engagement with the 'who bears the cost' question. The narrative is optimistic but lacks the normative depth required to address the trade-offs between individual voluntary action and systemic infrastructure changes.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage individual altruism to drive systemic change, but it obscures the structural inequalities inherent in mobility. By framing the solution as a voluntary 'walk or bike' day, it risks excluding low-income populations who rely on public transit for long commutes or those with physical disabilities, effectively shifting the burden of climate action onto the most vulnerable while expecting the government and corporations to provide the necessary infrastructure and flexibility without a clear cost-sharing mechanism.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes all citizens can walk or bike to work, ignoring those with disabilities, the elderly, or those living in areas with poor pedestrian infrastructure.
Equity of Burden: The plan expects companies to allow home office and the government to alter routes, but does not specify who bears the financial cost of these operational changes or the prizes.
Value Conflict: There is a tension between the 'voluntary' nature of the event and the proposal's expectation of government mandates on transportation routes and corporate work policies.
Data Privacy: The reliance on a Google-developed app to track citizen movement and generate 'Big Data' raises significant concerns about surveillance and data ownership, which are not addressed.

False Neutrality: The proposal presents the 'altruistic spirit' as the sole driver, masking the potential for greenwashing by sponsors who may benefit from the PR without making substantive operational changes.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet conversion, 1% solar law coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel government infrastructure overhauls and corporate home-office policies lacks a realistic implementation pathway or intermediate milestones.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' days and using apps to track carbon footprints is well-established in urban sustainability (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work). The proposal adds little new systemic insight, merely aggregating existing tactics without addressing the deeper structural barriers to long-term modal shift in high-density cities.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are tangible but negligible at a global scale, the projected long-term impacts are speculative and likely inflated. The proposal conflates correlation with causation, assuming that awareness generated by a single event will automatically result in complex legislative and infrastructural changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without detailing the political or economic mechanisms required to sustain these changes beyond the event.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative modeling, which is commendable. However, the logic connecting the event to the long-term outcomes is fragmented, and the reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute critical components (app development, policy changes) without a defined governance model weakens the overall narrative coherence.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term resilience perspective, this proposal risks being a 'feel-good' intervention that shifts the burden of systemic change onto individual behavior without addressing the structural rigidity of urban transport systems. The projected long-term impacts appear to be a wish list of policy outcomes rather than a grounded forecast of how a single awareness event could catalyze such deep, multi-sectoral transformation. True resilience requires durable infrastructure and policy shifts, not just temporary behavioral spikes.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term legislative outcomes (solar laws, electric bus mandates) is not substantiated; this assumes a level of political leverage the event likely does not possess.

The proposal assumes that 'awareness' alone is sufficient to overcome the high switching costs of changing daily commuting habits in a dense city without addressing infrastructure gaps (e.g., safety, distance, weather).

There is a risk of 'problem shifting' where the focus on walking/biking ignores the need for robust, reliable public transit that serves those who cannot walk or bike, potentially exacerbating inequities if public transport is not simultaneously improved.

The reliance on a single-day metric to predict 5-year systemic changes (recycling rates, energy laws) lacks a feedback loop or adaptive management strategy to ensure these habits persist after the event concludes.

A1.text: The peer review strongly validates my initial assessment of the 'awareness fallacy' and the lack of empirical causal mechanisms linking a single-day event to complex legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification). Specifically, A4's focus on the exclusionary nature of 'skipping the bus' and A5's analysis of equity burdens add critical dimensions to my technical critique, highlighting that the proposal's feasibility is compromised not just by data gaps but by social and accessibility constraints. While A2 and A6 reinforce the governance fragility, A4's point about existing tools (Strava/Google Fit) suggests the technical barrier is lower than I initially estimated, though the *integration* and *privacy* risks remain high. I maintain my original scores: the fundamental scientific uncertainty regarding the long-term impact projections (attributing macro-policy shifts to a micro-event) remains the primary disqualifier, regardless of the operational or equity refinements offered by peers.

A2.text: The pool strongly reinforces my initial assessment of the governance gap. A4's focus on the 'skip the bus' instruction adds a critical equity and safety dimension I noted but did not fully elaborate; mandating the abandonment of public transit for a one-day event is not just operationally risky but politically untenable in a city with significant mobility constraints. A5's point on the 'burden of altruism' further qualifies the feasibility score: expecting private firms to alter work policies and the government to re-route transit without a formal regulatory or incentive framework is a governance failure, not just a logistical one. The consensus on the 'awareness fallacy' (A1, A3, A6) confirms that the proposed causal link between a single event and complex legislative outcomes (solar law, bus electrification) lacks any institutional mechanism. While the intent is sound, the proposal remains a theoretical exercise rather than a governable policy intervention. No score revision is needed as the pool confirms the initial low feasibility and impact scores.

A3.text: The pool strongly validates my initial feasibility concerns, particularly regarding the operational fragility of a 'big bang' single-day event. A4's point on the 'skip the bus' instruction is critical; from an implementation risk lens, advising commuters to abandon public transit for walking without a robust safety and infrastructure audit creates a liability nightmare and excludes vulnerable populations, directly contradicting the proposal's equity goals. A5 and A6 correctly highlight that the 'awareness-to-policy' causal chain is a major adoption barrier; expecting a one-day event to trigger legislative changes (solar laws, bus electrification) without a dedicated lobbying or advocacy strategy is a fundamental strategic flaw. While the intent is sound, the execution plan lacks the necessary pilot phase, budget granularity, and risk mitigation for security/health logistics. The consensus on the inflated long-term impact projections confirms that the current scoring of 2 for feasibility and impact is appropriate and should not be revised.

A4.text: Reading the pool, I see strong alignment with my concerns about the 'skip the bus' directive and the digital divide. A1 and A6 correctly highlight the 'awareness fallacy'—expecting a single day to trigger complex legislation like solar laws. A5 adds a crucial equity lens, noting that this voluntary model shifts the burden of climate action onto those who can walk, while excluding the disabled and low-income who rely on transit. This reinforces my view that the proposal is not just technically unfeasible, but socially exclusionary. The 'Big Data' promise feels like a distraction from the lack of basic pedestrian safety and accessibility planning. I am revising my Impact score down to 2, as the potential for negative social friction (alienating non-walkers) and the negligible actual emissions reduction (due to low participation feasibility) outweigh the theoretical health benefits. The proposal risks being a 'feel-good' event that ignores the reality of the people it claims to serve.

A5.text: The pool powerfully reinforces my initial concerns regarding exclusion and the 'burden of altruism.' A4's specific critique of the 'skip the bus' instruction is crucial: it highlights how the proposal actively excludes low-income residents, the elderly, and those with disabilities who rely on public transit for long commutes, effectively punishing the most vulnerable for a 'voluntary' event. A1 and A6's dismantling of the causal link between a one-day event and complex legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification) confirms that the proposal hides a normative trade-off: it substitutes individual moral performance for necessary structural investment. The 'Big Data' reliance noted by A2 and A4 further exacerbates equity risks by creating a digital divide. While the health benefits are real, the proposal's framing obscures who bears the cost (time, safety, accessibility) versus who reaps the PR benefits (sponsors, government). I maintain my scores, as the fundamental ethical flaws regarding inclusion and the misattribution of systemic change to individual action remain unaddressed.

A6.text: The peer reviews powerfully reinforce my initial skepticism regarding the proposal's long-term resilience. A1 and A2 correctly identify the 'awareness fallacy'—the dangerous assumption that a single-day event can causally trigger complex legislative shifts (solar laws, bus electrification) without a defined political or economic mechanism. A4 and A5 add a critical equity dimension I must emphasize: the instruction to 'skip the bus' and rely on walking/biking risks excluding vulnerable populations (elderly, disabled, low-income) who depend on public transit, effectively shifting the burden of climate action onto those least able to bear it. This confirms that the proposal does not build systemic resilience but rather creates a fragile, temporary spike in behavior that may exacerbate social inequities. The consensus on the lack of a feedback loop for habit formation further validates my low impact score; without structural changes to infrastructure and policy, the 'long-term' impacts are merely speculative wish-listing. No score revision is needed as the pool confirms my initial assessment of the proposal's systemic fragility.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that 5% of the city's population (approx. 145,000 people) will voluntarily participate in a single-day event while simultaneously abandoning public transit (bus/subway) for walking or cycling. The causal mechanism for long-term impact (e.g., a 10% increase in recycling or the approval of solar laws) is tenuous; there is no empirical evidence provided that a one-day awareness event can drive complex legislative or behavioral shifts of this magnitude. Furthermore, the technical feasibility of the proposed app is low, as it requires integration with Google Maps and a custom GHG algorithm without a defined development roadmap or data privacy strategy.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is well-established globally, with numerous existing programs in the US, Canada, and Europe cited in the text itself. The proposal adds a layer of 'Big Data' tracking via a smartphone app, but the technical approach (using existing footprint algorithms) is not novel. The suggestion to skip public transit entirely in favor of walking for short distances is a standard behavioral nudge, not a systemic innovation.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact calculation (66.46 tCO2e/day) is mathematically consistent with the stated assumptions but likely overestimates actual emission reductions due to the 'rebound effect' and the unlikelihood of mass transit abandonment. The long-term impact projections are scientifically implausible; attributing a 10% increase in recycling, the passage of specific solar legislation, and the deployment of electric buses to a single awareness event represents a severe overestimation of causal efficacy. The extrapolation to a global scale (39 million tCO2e/year) is based on a linear scaling of a one-day event, which ignores systemic constraints and diminishing returns.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing for its claims. However, the presentation suffers from significant logical gaps and calculation errors (e.g., the long-term impact section conflates the impact of the event with the impact of unrelated policy changes like the solar law). The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') without textual explanation reduces the clarity of the argument. The tone is optimistic but lacks the rigorous hedging required for scientific plausibility.

A1.perspective_summary: From a systems and empirical evidence perspective, this proposal presents a classic case of 'awareness fallacy,' where a single event is expected to trigger complex, long-term systemic changes (legislation, infrastructure shifts) without a defined causal mechanism. While the short-term behavioral nudge is plausible, the quantitative impact projections are scientifically unsound due to unrealistic participation rates and the attribution of macro-policy outcomes to a micro-event. The technical implementation of the data tracking app is also underspecified.
key_concerns: The assumption that 5% of the population will abandon public transit for walking/cycling on a single day lacks empirical support and ignores urban mobility constraints.

The causal link between a one-day event and long-term policy outcomes (solar law approval, 10% recycling increase) is not scientifically established and likely represents a conflation of correlation and causation.
The 'Big Data' value proposition is vague; the proposal does not address data privacy, API costs, or the technical feasibility of real-time GHG calculation at scale.
The long-term impact calculations appear to attribute the success of broader city-wide initiatives (e.g., electric bus rollout) to this specific event, inflating the perceived impact.

The proposal suggests skipping public transit, which may inadvertently increase congestion if participants drive to the city center to walk, or simply fail to materialize due to weather, distance, or safety concerns.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on unrealistic institutional levers. It assumes the government can mandate or effectively coordinate 'home office' for private companies and modify public transport routes for a single day without a formal legislative or regulatory framework. Furthermore, the plan to have Google develop a custom app and manage all data collection is operationally speculative; large tech firms rarely commit resources to ad-hoc, one-off civic events without a commercial or strategic partnership. The governance capacity required to synchronize NGOs, media, transport authorities, and private employers for a single-day event is not demonstrated.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work Day). The proposal's novelty lies primarily in the specific application of a smartphone app for real-time GHG tracking and the attempt to link this data to long-term policy outcomes (like solar law adoption). However, the core mechanism is not innovative, and the governance model proposed (voluntary participation + government endorsement) is standard for such awareness campaigns.

impact.rationale: The projected long-term impacts are highly speculative and lack a causal governance link. The proposal forecasts a 10% increase in recycling and the approval of a distributed solar law as direct results of a single walking event. From a policy perspective, there is no mechanism described that translates 'awareness' into legislative change or systemic infrastructure shifts (e.g., electric bus implementation). The short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are negligible in the context of city-wide emissions, and the extrapolation to global impacts (39 million tCO2e/year) is mathematically flawed as it assumes a single-day behavioral change can be sustained and scaled globally without addressing structural barriers.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections (Purpose, Actions, Impacts, Costs), but the presentation of the governance strategy is weak. It lists stakeholders (Government, NGOs, Google, Media) but fails to define their specific roles, responsibilities, or the legal/contractual frameworks required to bind them. The reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'Main value generation loops v1.0.png') and the lack of a detailed implementation roadmap for the 'government authorization' phase reduce the professional quality of the submission.

A2.perspective_summary: This proposal presents a classic 'awareness campaign' that overestimates the power of a single event to drive systemic policy change. While the intent to promote active transport is sound, the governance model is fragile, relying on voluntary cooperation from powerful entities (Google, private employers) and assuming a direct causal link between a one-day event and complex legislative outcomes (solar laws, bus electrification). Without a robust institutional framework to enforce or incentivize participation and a realistic data strategy, the proposal remains a theoretical exercise rather than an actionable policy intervention.
key_concerns: Lack of a clear regulatory or contractual mechanism to secure 'home office' participation from private companies.
Unrealistic assumption that a single event can trigger long-term legislative changes (e.g., solar law approval) without a dedicated advocacy or lobbying strategy.
Dependency on Google for app development and data management without a confirmed partnership or funding model.
Absence of a risk management plan for public safety and traffic disruption during the event.
The 'Big Data' value proposition is vague; it is unclear how the government would legally access or utilize this data for urban planning without privacy regulations.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'big bang' single-day event model with unrealistic dependencies: government authorization to modify bus routes, Google developing a custom tracking app, and mass corporate adoption of home office for a one-off day. The operational complexity of coordinating security, health support, and media for a city-wide walk without a clear pilot phase or budget breakdown makes immediate execution highly risky.

novelty.rationale: The core concept (walking/biking to work days) is a well-established global practice (e.g., 'Bike to Work Day'). The proposal adds little novelty beyond aggregating existing ideas (Ecobici, home office) and proposing a generic tracking app. The 'business model' is essentially a sponsorship drive, which is standard for such events.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact is negligible (66 tCO2e/day) and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e/year) are mathematically flawed due to unrealistic extrapolation assumptions (e.g., assuming a one-day event triggers permanent 10% recycling increases and 2% electric bus fleet conversion). The proposal conflates a single event's awareness with systemic infrastructure changes that require years of policy work, not a single day of walking.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but the data analysis is superficial and the logic connecting the event to long-term policy outcomes is weak. The inclusion of 'Business Model Canvas' and 'Review of Objections' shows effort, but the reliance on image placeholders (e.g., 'main-objections.png') and unverified assumptions undermines the professional presentation.

A3.perspective_summary: From an implementation standpoint, this proposal is a classic 'awareness event' that overestimates its ability to drive systemic change. While the intent is positive, the operational plan is fragile, relying on high-level government and corporate cooperation that is unlikely to materialize for a single day without a proven track record. The financial and logistical burden of security, health support, and app development for a one-off event is high relative to the minimal, short-lived emissions reduction.
key_concerns: The proposal assumes government will modify public transport routes and companies will mandate home office for a single day without a clear incentive structure or legal framework.
The long-term impact calculations are mathematically unsound, projecting permanent policy changes (e.g., electric bus adoption) as a direct result of a one-day awareness event.
There is no clear budget or funding strategy beyond vague 'sponsorships' and 'government support,' making the cost of security, health personnel, and app development a major risk.
The reliance on a custom app developed by Google (or similar) for tracking is a significant technical and partnership barrier that is not addressed with a realistic timeline or cost estimate.
The proposal lacks a pilot phase; scaling from a single day to a global movement without testing the operational model in a smaller context is a high-risk strategy.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a 'government authorization' to modify bus routes and mandate home office days, which is a massive bureaucratic hurdle for a single-day event. Furthermore, the requirement for a custom Google-developed app to track specific routes and calculate GHG savings creates a high technical barrier that ignores existing, accessible tools (like Strava or Google Fit) that already serve this purpose, likely excluding those without smartphones or data plans.

novelty.rationale: The concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice. While the proposal attempts to gamify it with prizes and data tracking, the core mechanism is not novel. The specific claim of using 'big data' to redesign subway entrances is a standard urban planning outcome of existing mobility studies, not a unique innovation of this event.

impact.rationale: The short-term impact of a single day of walking is negligible (66 tCO2e) and the long-term projections (39 million tCO2e) rely on highly optimistic, unproven assumptions about habit formation (e.g., 10% recycling increase, 5% solar adoption) triggered by one event. However, the potential for immediate health benefits and localized awareness in Buenos Aires is real, even if the global scaling claims are inflated.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is fragmented and contains significant logical gaps. It explicitly asks people to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' to walk, which contradicts the goal of sustainable transport for those living far away. The text is riddled with typos ('kilometrers', 'aligorithm'), inconsistent formatting, and a confusing flow between the 'event' and 'long-term policy changes' that are not directly caused by the event itself.

A4.perspective_summary: From the ground, this proposal feels like a top-down campaign that misunderstands the daily realities of Buenos Aires residents. While the intent to promote walking is positive, the instruction to 'skip the bus' is dangerous and impractical for anyone with a commute longer than a few blocks. The heavy reliance on a custom app and government mandates ignores the accessibility needs of the elderly, disabled, and low-income populations who cannot easily walk long distances or afford smartphones. The proposal risks alienating the very people it aims to help by setting unrealistic expectations for a one-day event to solve systemic transport and climate issues.
key_concerns: The instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is exclusionary and potentially unsafe for residents with long commutes, disabilities, or mobility issues.
The proposal assumes universal smartphone ownership and data access for the tracking app, creating a digital divide that excludes vulnerable populations.
The reliance on government mandates for home office and route changes is a major feasibility risk that is not addressed with a contingency plan.
The long-term impact projections (e.g., 10% recycling increase) are causally disconnected from a single-day walking event and lack a realistic behavioral change mechanism.
The proposal does not account for safety concerns (traffic, crime) for pedestrians in specific neighborhoods of Buenos Aires, which could deter participation.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unverified assumption that the government will mandate or strongly enforce 'home office' and route modifications for public transport, which contradicts the voluntary nature of the event. Furthermore, the logistical burden of providing security and health support for a city-wide walking event without a clear operational plan for crowd control or emergency response in a dense urban environment like Buenos Aires presents significant execution risks.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice, not a novel innovation. While the integration of a specific app for tracking and the 'Big Data' angle for urban planning adds a layer of modernity, the fundamental behavioral intervention and the value generation loops are derivative of existing campaigns (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work programs cited in the text).

impact.rationale: The proposal identifies clear potential benefits (GHG reduction, health improvements, traffic flow), but the projected long-term impacts (e.g., 39 million tCO2e/year globally) are based on a linear extrapolation of a single-day event that ignores behavioral decay and systemic barriers. The immediate impact is real but likely limited to the 5% participation rate, and the proposal fails to address the 'rebound effect' or the exclusion of those who physically cannot walk or bike, limiting the net positive impact on equity.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to use data, but it suffers from significant inconsistencies in its calculations (e.g., the jump from 1% to 5% solar coverage in the long-term section without explanation) and a lack of critical engagement with the 'who bears the cost' question. The narrative is optimistic but lacks the normative depth required to address the trade-offs between individual voluntary action and systemic infrastructure changes.

A5.perspective_summary: This proposal attempts to leverage individual altruism to drive systemic change, but it obscures the structural inequalities inherent in mobility. By framing the solution as a voluntary 'walk or bike' day, it risks excluding low-income populations who rely on public transit for long commutes or those with physical disabilities, effectively shifting the burden of climate action onto the most vulnerable while expecting the government and corporations to provide the necessary infrastructure and flexibility without a clear cost-sharing mechanism.
key_concerns: Exclusion Risk: The proposal assumes all citizens can walk or bike to work, ignoring those with disabilities, the elderly, or those living in areas with poor pedestrian infrastructure.
Equity of Burden: The plan expects companies to allow home office and the government to alter routes, but does not specify who bears the financial cost of these operational changes or the prizes.
Value Conflict: There is a tension between the 'voluntary' nature of the event and the proposal's expectation of government mandates on transportation routes and corporate work policies.
Data Privacy: The reliance on a Google-developed app to track citizen movement and generate 'Big Data' raises significant concerns about surveillance and data ownership, which are not addressed.

False Neutrality: The proposal presents the 'altruistic spirit' as the sole driver, masking the potential for greenwashing by sponsors who may benefit from the PR without making substantive operational changes.

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on a highly optimistic and unproven causal chain where a single-day awareness event triggers systemic policy shifts (e.g., 2% electric bus fleet conversion, 1% solar law coverage) and behavioral changes (10% recycling increase) over five years. The assumption that a one-off 'Walk Day' will compel government infrastructure overhauls and corporate home-office policies lacks a realistic implementation pathway or intermediate milestones.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' days and using apps to track carbon footprints is well-established in urban sustainability (e.g., Earth Hour, Bike to Work). The proposal adds little new systemic insight, merely aggregating existing tactics without addressing the deeper structural barriers to long-term modal shift in high-density cities.

impact.rationale: While the short-term emission savings (66 tCO2e/day) are tangible but negligible at a global scale, the projected long-term impacts are speculative and likely inflated. The proposal conflates correlation with causation, assuming that awareness generated by a single event will automatically result in complex legislative and infrastructural changes (solar laws, electric bus fleets) without detailing the political or economic mechanisms required to sustain these changes beyond the event.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts quantitative modeling, which is commendable. However, the logic connecting the event to the long-term outcomes is fragmented, and the reliance on external entities (Google, Government) to execute critical components (app development, policy changes) without a defined governance model weakens the overall narrative coherence.

A6.perspective_summary: From a long-term resilience perspective, this proposal risks being a 'feel-good' intervention that shifts the burden of systemic change onto individual behavior without addressing the structural rigidity of urban transport systems. The projected long-term impacts appear to be a wish list of policy outcomes rather than a grounded forecast of how a single awareness event could catalyze such deep, multi-sectoral transformation. True resilience requires durable infrastructure and policy shifts, not just temporary behavioral spikes.
key_concerns: The causal link between a one-day event and long-term legislative outcomes (solar laws, electric bus mandates) is not substantiated; this assumes a level of political leverage the event likely does not possess.

The proposal assumes that 'awareness' alone is sufficient to overcome the high switching costs of changing daily commuting habits in a dense city without addressing infrastructure gaps (e.g., safety, distance, weather).

There is a risk of 'problem shifting' where the focus on walking/biking ignores the need for robust, reliable public transit that serves those who cannot walk or bike, potentially exacerbating inequities if public transport is not simultaneously improved.

The reliance on a single-day metric to predict 5-year systemic changes (recycling rates, energy laws) lacks a feedback loop or adaptive management strategy to ensure these habits persist after the event concludes.

clusters.label: The 'Awareness Fallacy' and Causal Overreach
clusters.summary: A strong consensus across all experts that the proposal fundamentally misunderstands the causal mechanisms required to drive systemic change. The experts uniformly reject the premise that a single-day awareness event can directly trigger complex, long-term legislative outcomes (e.g., solar law approval, electric bus fleet conversion) or significant behavioral shifts (e.g., 10% recycling increase) without intermediate policy, advocacy, or infrastructure interventions. This cluster highlights the 'awareness fallacy' where micro-events are incorrectly attributed macro-policy impacts.

clusters.label: Equity, Accessibility, and the 'Skip the Bus' Directive
clusters.summary: Experts A4 and A5, supported by A1 and A6, raise critical concerns regarding the proposal's exclusionary nature. The specific instruction to 'skip the bus' and 'avoid the subway' is identified as dangerous and impractical for vulnerable populations (elderly, disabled, low-income) and those with long commutes. This cluster focuses on the social risk of shifting the burden of climate action onto those with the least mobility options, creating a digital divide via the app requirement and potentially exacerbating social inequities rather than solving them.

clusters.label: Governance Fragility and Operational Unrealism
clusters.summary: The pool identifies a severe lack of a realistic governance model. Experts note that the proposal relies on unverified assumptions of government mandates (modifying bus routes, enforcing home office) and corporate cooperation without a defined legal, regulatory, or financial framework. The dependency on a custom Google-developed app and the absence of a pilot phase, budget breakdown, or risk management plan for security and health in a dense urban environment render the operational plan fragile and likely unexecutable.

clusters.label: Technical Implementation and Data Strategy Gaps
clusters.summary: While the concept of tracking via an app is noted, experts point out significant technical and strategic gaps. The proposal fails to address data privacy, API costs, and the feasibility of real-time GHG calculation at scale. Furthermore, experts suggest that relying on a custom app is unnecessary given existing tools (Strava, Google Fit), and the 'Big Data' value proposition is vague, lacking a clear strategy for how the data would be legally accessed or utilized for urban planning without violating privacy regulations.

open_questions: What specific, intermediate policy mechanisms or advocacy strategies could realistically bridge the gap between a one-day awareness event and the passage of complex legislation like the distributed solar law?
How can the proposal be redesigned to ensure inclusivity for residents with disabilities, the elderly, and those with long commutes, moving away from the exclusionary 'skip the bus' directive?

What is the concrete governance framework (legal, financial, or regulatory) required to secure voluntary participation from private corporations for home office and from the government for route modifications?
How can the 'Big Data' component be restructured to address data privacy concerns and leverage existing, accessible tools rather than requiring a custom, unproven app development partnership?
What pilot phase or iterative testing strategy is necessary to validate the operational feasibility and safety of a city-wide walking event before attempting a 'big bang' launch?
How can the proposal distinguish between the direct impact of the event and the indirect, long-term systemic changes to avoid the 'awareness fallacy' in its impact modeling?

feasibility.rationale: The proposal relies on highly optimistic and unverified assumptions regarding government mandates (modifying bus routes, enforcing home office) and corporate cooperation without a defined legal or financial framework. The operational plan is fragile, lacking a pilot phase, budget breakdown, or risk management for security and health in a dense urban environment. Furthermore, the technical dependency on a custom Google-developed app ignores existing accessible tools and presents significant data privacy and cost barriers.

novelty.rationale: The core concept of 'Walk to Work' or 'Bike to Work' days is a well-established global practice with numerous existing precedents. While the proposal attempts to add a layer of 'Big Data' tracking via a smartphone app, the technical approach is derivative, and the governance model (voluntary participation + government endorsement) is standard for awareness campaigns. The proposal aggregates existing ideas without offering a systemic innovation or a unique mechanism for behavioral change.

impact.rationale: The short-term emission savings are mathematically consistent with stated assumptions but likely overestimated due to the unlikelihood of mass transit abandonment and the 'rebound effect.' The long-term impact projections are scientifically implausible, as they attribute complex legislative outcomes (solar law approval, electric bus deployment) and significant behavioral shifts (10% recycling increase) to a single-day awareness event. This represents a severe 'awareness fallacy' where micro-events are incorrectly credited with macro-policy impacts.

presentation.rationale: The proposal is structured with clear sections and attempts to provide quantitative backing, which is commendable. However, the presentation suffers from significant logical gaps, calculation errors (conflating event impact with unrelated policy changes), and reliance on image placeholders without textual explanation. The tone is overly optimistic and lacks the rigorous hedging required for scientific plausibility, while the text contains typos and inconsistent formatting.

final.synthesis: The final judgment integrates a unanimous consensus from all six experts (A1-A6) that the proposal suffers from a fundamental 'awareness fallacy.' All experts agree that the causal link between a one-day event and long-term systemic changes (legislation, infrastructure) is non-existent without intermediate policy mechanisms. The synthesis also incorporates specific concerns raised by A4 and A5 regarding equity and accessibility, noting that the directive to 'skip the bus' is exclusionary and potentially dangerous for vulnerable populations. While the proposal's structure (A1, A3, A6) was noted as a positive, the operational fragility (A2, A3, A5) and technical gaps (A1, A4) regarding the app development and data strategy were weighted heavily to lower the feasibility and novelty scores. The impact score was reduced to reflect the consensus that the long-term projections are speculative and mathematically flawed due to linear extrapolation of a single-day event.
final.uncertainty: The primary uncertainty remains the potential for the proposal to serve as a successful pilot for a smaller-scale, localized event if the 'skip the bus' directive is removed and the scope is narrowed to short-distance walking only. However, the current proposal's reliance on high-level government mandates and a custom app partnership creates a binary outcome: either these specific, unlikely conditions are met, or the project fails entirely. There is also uncertainty regarding whether the 'Big Data' component could be salvaged by integrating with existing open-source mobility platforms rather than requiring a custom Google partnership, which would alter the feasibility assessment.
